channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Juno takes temperature of Jupiter's fiery moon Io - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Juno takes temperature of Jupiter's fiery moon Io  Phys.org
  2. NASA’s Juno Takes Temperature of Jupiter’s Fiery Moon Io  NASA (.gov)
  3. Jupiter's moon Io is the most volcanically active body in the solar system, with hundreds of active volcanoes that can shoot sulphur plumes more than 500 kilometres above its surface — a landscape so extreme that early Voyager scientists initially mistook the p  Space Daily
  4. Juno’s Microwave Vision Peers beneath Surface of Jupiter’s Volcanic Moon Io  Sci.News
  5. Gravitational Tug-of-War Turns up the Heat on Jupiter's Moon Io, Causing Underground Temperatures to Soar  Discover Magazine

item.title: Finding no evidence of life in Enceladus’s ocean could be almost as scientifically important as finding it — because a world with liquid water, internal heat, phosphorus and complex organic chemistry but no detectable biology would suggest that the ingredient - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Finding no evidence of life in Enceladus’s ocean could be almost as scientifically important as finding it — because a world with liquid water, internal heat, phosphorus and complex organic chemistry but no detectable biology would suggest that the ingredient  Space Daily
  2. Why Europa’s Hidden Ocean May Be More Difficult to Reach Than Scientists Thought | Newswise  Newswise
  3. Jupiter's Moon Europa Holds Hidden Ocean With Potential Clues About Life Beyond Earth  NDTV
  4. The case for Enceladus as a potentially habitable world does not rest on speculation alone: sodium salts point to an ocean that has interacted with rock, molecular hydrogen reveals a potential chemical energy source, and phosphorus—the long-missing essen  Space Daily
  5. Enceladus, a Saturn moon just 500 kilometres wide, fires geysers of water thousands of kilometres into space through cracks at its south pole — and that water carries the building blocks of life  Space Daily

item.title: How Fast Is the Universe Really Expanding? - Quanta Magazine
item.description:
1. How Fast Is the Universe Really Expanding?  Quanta Magazine
  2. The observable universe is roughly 93 billion light-years across despite being only 13.8 billion years old, because space kept expanding while the oldest light was travelling toward us  Space Daily
  3. The Universe Can Expand Faster Than Light Without Breaking Physics  SciTechDaily

item.title: The gold in your wedding ring might an origin far more violent than you think: much of the universe’s gold was likely forged when two neutron stars collided — an event bright enough to outshine hundreds of millions of Suns for days, in a burst astronomers call - Space Daily

item.title: Later this decade NASA plans to launch Dragonfly, a car-sized nuclear-powered drone that will fly from site to site across Saturn's moon Titan - the only world besides Earth with rivers, lakes and rain, though there the rain is liquid methane - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Later this decade NASA plans to launch Dragonfly, a car-sized nuclear-powered drone that will fly from site to site across Saturn's moon Titan - the only world besides Earth with rivers, lakes and rain, though there the rain is liquid methane  Space Daily

  3. NASA Embraces Challenges Of Saturn Moon Mission  Aviation Week
  4. NASA Livestreams Dragonfly Assembly Today as the Titan Rotorcraft Takes Shape  Glitchwire

item.title: After nearly 30 years, NASA realized this near-Earth asteroid is actually a comet - Space
item.description:
1. After nearly 30 years, NASA realized this near-Earth asteroid is actually a comet  Space
  2. NASA Study Finds Near-Earth Asteroid Is Actually Comet  NASA (.gov)
  3. A near-Earth asteroid tracked since 1998 turned up far from its predicted spot in the sky, and astronomers have now confirmed why — it is secretly a comet, nudged off its path by escaping gas, with a tail too faint for ordinary telescopes to see  Space Daily
  4. Near-Earth asteroid not an asteroid at all  Popular Science
  5. Astronomers Catch a "Dark Comet" Red-Handed  Universe Today

item.title: The computers guiding Voyager 1 through interstellar space process roughly 8,000 instructions per second; a modern smartphone processes more than 14 billion — yet that 1970s machine has operated for nearly half a century, farther from Earth than any spa - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The computers guiding Voyager 1 through interstellar space process roughly 8,000 instructions per second; a modern smartphone processes more than 14 billion — yet that 1970s machine has operated for nearly half a century, farther from Earth than any spa  Space Daily
  2. A glimpse of home was among the last images Voyager 1’s cameras ever sent. Just 34 minutes after taking the most distant photo of Earth ever, the cameras were powered off to conserve energy — going dark for good  Space Daily
  3. NASA has calculated, to the precise second, when Voyager 1 will be exactly one light-day from Earth: 2:16 a.m. on 18 November 2026, at a distance of 16,094,799,096 miles — a number that would have been science fiction when the probe launched in 1977  Space Daily
  4. NASA's Voyager 1 Radio Transmitter Is Weaker Than a Refrigerator Lightbulb. It Still Reaches Earth From 15 Billion Miles Away  19FortyFive
  5. Voyager 1 on track to reach one light-day from Earth  it-online.co.za

item.title: Volcanoes Started Earth’s ‘Great Dying.’ This Unexpected Plant Made It Worse - Gizmodo
item.description:

  4. Fern-Fueled Wildfires Raged across Europe for Millennia during End-Triassic Mass Extinction  Sci.News
  5. Fires raged across Europe during “extreme global warming” 201 million years ago  IslanderNews.com

item.title: Scientists think at least two of Uranus's moons — Titania and Oberon — may harbour liquid water oceans beneath their icy crusts, kept warm not by the sun but by radioactive decay deep in their interiors. If true, they join a growing list of ocean worlds in the sol - Space Daily
item.description: Scientists think at least two of Uranus's moons — Titania and Oberon — may harbour liquid water oceans beneath their icy crusts, kept warm not by the sun but by radioactive decay deep in their interiors. If true, they join a growing list of ocean worlds in the sol  Space Daily

item.title: Cats sleep between 12 and 16 hours a day not because they are lazy but because their hunting strategy requires explosive short-burst energy — and a body that can sprint at nearly 50 kilometres per hour needs most of the day to prepare for a few seconds of - Space Daily

item.title: How does a planet 4.5 billion kilometres from the Sun, where daylight is roughly 900 times dimmer than on Earth, whip up the fastest winds in the entire solar system? - Space Daily
item.description:
1. How does a planet 4.5 billion kilometres from the Sun, where daylight is roughly 900 times dimmer than on Earth, whip up the fastest winds in the entire solar system?  Space Daily
  2. Neptune has the strongest winds in the solar system, gusting past 1,200 miles an hour on a planet that gets barely a fraction of the sunlight Earth does  Space Daily

item.title: A black bear's heart drops from roughly 55 beats per minute to as few as 8 during hibernation, yet it emerges in spring with almost no muscle or bone loss: a biological trick space agencies are studying to protect astronauts on long missions - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A black bear's heart drops from roughly 55 beats per minute to as few as 8 during hibernation, yet it emerges in spring with almost no muscle or bone loss: a biological trick space agencies are studying to protect astronauts on long missions  Space Daily

  4. Nasa explored putting astronauts into a hibernation-like state for Mars missions, but scientists are still trying to make the science-fiction idea safe for humans  The Times of India

item.title: Astronauts returning from six-month missions describe a persistent 'observer' sensation — the feeling of watching their own lives from a half-step outside the frame, weeks after they're back on the ground - spacewar.com
item.description:
1. Astronauts returning from six-month missions describe a persistent 'observer' sensation — the feeling of watching their own lives from a half-step outside the frame, weeks after they're back on the ground  spacewar.com
  2. NASA debriefs and crew psychologists hear it often enough to treat it as normal readjustment: astronauts back from long missions describe observing their own lives rather than fully taking part in them  Space Daily
  3. Why the smell of rain feels overwhelming to astronauts after spending months in space, according to Nasa  The Times of India
  4. Astronauts returning from long stays in orbit say the smell of rain is almost too much to bear and a familiar street feels like something they are piloting rather than driving  Space Daily
  5. Astronauts who have seen the whole Earth at once describe the overview effect — every war and border and argument hanging in silence — and afterwards traffic and small talk can feel strangely far away, like the world seen through glass  Space Daily
